text: The camera angle is positioned thirty degrees downward, showing the closure of the posterior defect with one larger gap and several smaller gaps. The right posterior rectus sheath is visible as a white structure on the far side of the screen. In the ETEP Reef Stoppa technique, the posterior rectus sheath edges are not closed together due to tension, instead using the peritoneum and pseudosac to bridge the gap.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-lock 180 suture to sew the posterior sheath to the peritoneum, carefully avoiding the small bowel that is positioned against the peritoneum.

During the NETEP procedure, the surgeon carefully navigates intra-abdominal adhesions, removing only enough to facilitate the surgical task. The suturing process involves differentiating between fatty omentum, preperitoneal fat, neoperitoneum, and adhesions.
The surgeon carefully repairs each visible defect to prevent the formation of an interparietal hernia, ensuring that all gaps are closed securely.

The surgeon searches for smaller defects in the cephalad portion of the space. The left posterior rectus sheath is visible as a white structure on the right side of the screen, with a gap in the peritoneum medial to it. The small bowel is visible underneath the peritoneum, requiring careful suturing to avoid injury.

The surgeon harvests the hernia sac, utilizing the patient's native tissue during the preperitoneal dissection. Upon reaching the hernia sac, the surgeon examines its contents, which may include true peritoneum or a pseudosac, to identify usable tissue.

The video shows a large defect with multiple Swiss cheese defects and diastasis in the patient's abdominal wall. The surgeon assesses the tissue as unhealthy and in need of repair. Despite the large defect, the surgeon decides against performing a TAR, noting that the abdominal wall is lax and the posterior sheath release has effectively released the linea alba and anterior component.

The surgeon considers adding a unilateral TAR if excessive tension is observed during closure. An OV lock, 180, 18-inch suture is used, with the repair's strength relying on the wide overlap of the mesh rather than the suture itself.

The surgeon takes small bites of the hernia pseudosac to avoid forming a large seroma, which is more symptomatic than multiple small seromas. The dome of the hernia is high, prompting the surgeon to lower the pneumoperitoneum to about ten to facilitate reaching and grasping the pseudosac. The pressure is further reduced as needed to close the defect more easily.

The surgeon demonstrates a technique for closing larger defects by laying out multiple sutures without tightening them initially. The sutures are then progressively tensioned together, similar to tightening a corset, to minimize the risk of breaking and to manage the length of the eighteen-inch suture effectively.
The surgeon demonstrates progressive tensioning of sutures to maintain visibility of the ipsilateral linea alba, which is challenging to see due to the narrow rectus muscle.

The surgeon takes a substantial bite of the linea alba, incorporating both the anterior rectus sheath and the cut posterior rectus sheath veil. The posterior rectus sheath is cut approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba, enhancing its visibility. The surgeon pulls the suture until resistance is felt before proceeding to the next suture.

The surgeon closes the defect, initially encountering little resistance, but later avoids fully tightening due to increased resistance. Small bites are taken from the hernia pseudosac to reduce the size of the seroma.
The surgeon parks the needle before placing the next suture to maintain organization and prevent confusion when dealing with multiple sutures.
The surgeon progressively tensions the suture, pulling it until slight resistance is felt, allowing for continued use of the same suture to close the linea alba and the pseudosac.

The surgeon runs out of suture and applies additional tension to the existing suture. If a significant defect remains, a new suture is introduced. The final pull involves pulling the suture towards the surgeon while using the left hand to brace the tissue.

The surgeon pulls the suture with tension, ensuring it does not cut into the ipsilateral linea alba. The needle driver briefly comes off the suture due to excessive pulling. The ipsilateral abdominal wall is expected to medialize, while the fixed ports require occasional adjustment by the bedside assistant.

The surgeon shifts the ipsilateral abdominal wall medially to align it with the contralateral side while closing the defect. The needle is kept in the right hand or parked to allow two-handed manipulation of the sutures. The final pull is directed towards the surgeon, with the left hand bracing the tissue.

The surgeon moves towards the cephalad portion of the defect closure, adjusting hand positions for efficient needle driving. The left hand is positioned at a favorable angle for suturing, highlighting the use of image inversion to enhance efficiency.
The surgeon uses image inversion to turn the visual field upside down, allowing the left hand to function as the right hand and vice versa, optimizing the angle for suturing with the dominant hand.
The surgeon uses robotic suturing to efficiently tighten the last section of the suture, preparing to perform an image inversion.

The bedside assistant swaps the instruments, visibly changing their positions on the screen. Instrument four is now positioned between instruments one and three, while instrument two is to the right of instrument three. The image inversion results in the roof appearing as the floor.
The surgeon uses a needle driver in the right hand, achieving a better angle for suturing after image inversion, allowing for more efficient and faster suturing.

The surgeon sutures the defect, initially right side up, then using image inversion for longer sections. The suturing involves taking bites of the Linea Alba, incorporating both the anterior and posterior rectal sheaths. Due to the narrow diastasis, bites are not taken in the center.
The surgeon progressively tensions the suture without over-tightening, using the width of the gap to manage suture length. With the image inverted, the defect is sutured together efficiently.

The surgeon uses a needle driver to apply progressive tension to the suture, ensuring it does not pop off while closing a nine-centimeter defect. The defect is visibly brought together without requiring a unilateral or bilateral tar.

The surgeon assesses the width of the rectus muscle, noting it is twice the width of the defect, suggesting closure without a transversus abdominis release (TAR). The defect is closed by running the suture line back, grabbing the veil of the posterior rectus sheath, and ensuring the closure is secure despite any tension.

The surgeon performs additional throws to lock the stitch, running the suture back almost entirely due to ease. As the suture length diminishes, a few bites are taken to secure the stitch. The image is reverted to right side up to complete the remaining steps of the procedure.

The surgeon rotates the scope 180 degrees at the console to revert the image orientation. The surgical field is measured for mesh placement, focusing on the floor of the defect. The mesh, approximately 32 centimeters long, is prepared to cover the defect, with additional length added to accommodate potential stretching.
The surgeon places a Pry-Teen mesh sheet measuring thirty by forty-five centimeters into the surgical space through an eight millimeter port, demonstrating that upsizing the port is unnecessary.

The surgeon places the mesh under the diaphragm, ensuring it overlaps adequately above the diaphragm. The mesh appears slightly redundant due to a mis-measurement, showing folds and doubling on itself, but its lightweight nature allows for this adjustment.

The surgeon decides not to trim the oversized mesh within the abdominal cavity, allowing it to fold on itself due to its lightweight nature. Scissors are visible as a potential tool for trimming the mesh if needed.
